tools.description: Add a comment to an existing GitHub issue, pull request, or discussion. Use this to provide feedback, answer questions, or add information to an existing conversation. For creating new items, use create_issue, create_discussion, or create_pull_request instead. IMPORTANT: Comments are subject to validation constraints enforced by the MCP server - maximum 65536 characters for the complete comment (including footer which is added automatically), 10 mentions (@username), and 50 links. Exceeding these limits will result in an immediate error with specific guidance. NOTE: By default, this tool requires discussions:write permission. If your GitHub App lacks Discussions permission, set 'discussions: false' in the workflow's safe-outputs.add-comment configuration to exclude this permission. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 1 comment(s) can be added.

body.description: The comment text in Markdown format. This is the 'body' field - do not use 'comment_body' or other variations. Provide helpful, relevant information that adds value to the conversation. CONSTRAINTS: The complete comment (your body text + automatically added footer) must not exceed 65536 characters total. Maximum 10 mentions (@username), maximum 50 links (http/https URLs). A footer (~200-500 characters) is automatically appended with workflow attribution, so leave adequate space. If these limits are exceeded, the tool call will fail with a detailed error message indicating which constraint was violated.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

item_number.description: The issue, pull request, or discussion number to comment on. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 123 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/123). If omitted, the tool auto-targets the issue, PR, or discussion that triggered this workflow. Auto-targeting only works for issue, pull_request, discussion, and comment event triggers — it does NOT work for schedule, workflow_dispatch, push, or workflow_run triggers. For those trigger types, always provide item_number explicitly, or the comment will be silently discarded.

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: add_comment

tools.description: Add labels to an existing GitHub issue or pull request for categorization and filtering. Labels must already exist in the repository. For creating new issues with labels, use create_issue with the labels property instead. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 5 label(s) can be added.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

item_number.description: Issue or PR number to add labels to. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 456 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/456). If omitted, adds labels to the issue or PR that triggered this workflow. Only works for issue or pull_request event triggers. For schedule, workflow_dispatch, or other triggers, item_number is required — omitting it will silently skip the label operation.

labels.description: Label names to add (e.g., ['bug', 'priority-high']). Labels must exist in the repository.

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: add_labels

tools.description: Report that a tool or capability needed to complete the task is not available, or share any information you deem important about missing functionality or limitations. Use this when you cannot accomplish what was requested because the required functionality is missing or access is restricted.

alternatives.description: Any workarounds, manual steps, or alternative approaches the user could take (max 256 characters).

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

reason.description: Explanation of why this tool is needed or what information you want to share about the limitation (max 256 characters).

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tool.description: Optional: Name or description of the missing tool or capability (max 128 characters). Be specific about what functionality is needed.

tools.name: missing_tool

tools.description: Log a transparency message when no significant actions are needed. Use this to confirm workflow completion and provide visibility when analysis is complete but no changes or outputs are required (e.g., 'No issues found', 'All checks passed'). This ensures the workflow produces human-visible output even when no other actions are taken.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

message.description: Status or completion message to log. Should explain what was analyzed and the outcome (e.g., 'Code review complete - no issues found', 'Analysis complete - all tests passing').

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: noop

tools.description: Report that data or information needed to complete the task is not available. Use this when you cannot accomplish what was requested because required data, context, or information is missing.

alternatives.description: Any workarounds, manual steps, or alternative approaches the user could take (max 256 characters).

context.description: Additional context about the missing data or where it should come from (max 256 characters).

data_type.description: Type or description of the missing data or information (max 128 characters). Be specific about what data is needed.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

reason.description: Explanation of why this data is needed to complete the task (max 256 characters).

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: missing_data

annotations.title: Get a specific label from a repository.

tools.description: Get a specific label from a repository.
tools.name: get_label

tools.description: Get information about a specific issue in a GitHub repository.

method.description:
The read operation to perform on a single issue.
Options are:
1. get - Get details of a specific issue.
2. get_comments - Get issue comments.
3. get_sub_issues - Get sub-issues of the issue.
4. get_labels - Get labels assigned to the issue.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

tools.name: issue_read

tools.description: List supported issue types for repository owner (organization).

owner.description: The organization owner of the repository

tools.name: list_issue_types

tools.description: List issues in a GitHub repository. For pagination, use the 'endCursor' from the previous response's 'pageInfo' in the 'after' parameter.

after.description: Cursor for pagination. Use the endCursor from the previous page's PageInfo for GraphQL APIs.

direction.description: Order direction. If provided, the 'orderBy' also needs to be provided.

orderBy.description: Order issues by field. If provided, the 'direction' also needs to be provided.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

state.description: Filter by state, by default both open and closed issues are returned when not provided

tools.name: list_issues

tools.description: Search for issues in GitHub repositories using issues search syntax already scoped to is:issue

owner.description: Optional repository owner. If provided with repo, only issues for this repository are listed.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

query.description: Search query using GitHub issues search syntax

repo.description: Optional repository name. If provided with owner, only issues for this repository are listed.

sort.description: Sort field by number of matches of categories, defaults to best match

tools.name: search_issues

content.text:
{"number":56986,"title":"Azure.Identity: Support programmatic Transport and Policy customization for ConfigurableCredential and ChainedTokenCredential","body":"Currently, credentials created via `IConfiguration` (through `ConfigurableCredential` and `ChainedTokenCredential`) are constructed entirely from config — there is no hook point for developers to programmatically set `Transport` or add `HttpPipelinePolicy` instances to credential options.

This is by design for the config-driven creation path, but creates a gap for scenarios where developers need to:

1. **Override Transport** — e.g., inject a custom `HttpClientTransport` backed by `IHttpClientFactory`, or use a corporate proxy transport
2. **Add policies** — e.g., logging policies, telemetry policies, or custom retry policies that can't be expressed as config values
3. **Apply to chained credentials** — each source in a `ChainedTokenCredential` has its own options, so customizations need to reach individual credential instances inside the chain

### Current Behavior

- `ClientOptions(IConfigurationSection)` always creates a default transport via `HttpPipelineTransport.Create()` — config has no transport binding
- `HttpPipelineTransport` is abstract and cannot be instantiated from config
- `HttpPipelinePolicy` instances are added via `AddPolicy()` — a programmatic-only API
- `ChainedTokenCredentialFactory` creates credentials directly from `DefaultAzureCredentialOptions` source elements with no parent propagation of Transport or policies

### Desired Behavior

Provide a mechanism for developers to customize Transport and/or add policies to credentials created from configuration. This should work for:

- Single credentials (e.g., `CredentialSource: "AzureCliCredential"`)
- `ChainedTokenCredential` with multiple sources

### Design Considerations

- Should this be a callback/delegate on `DefaultAzureCredentialOptions` that receives each credential's options before construction?
- Should there be a post-config `Action` that applies to all credentials in the chain?
- How does this interact with DI — should `IHttpClientFactory` integration be a first-class pattern?
- `Transport` is not serializable to JSON, so this is inherently a code + config hybrid scenario","state":"open","html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/issues/56986","user":{"login":"m-nash","id":64171366,"profile_url":"https://github.com/m-nash","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/64171366?v=4"},"author_association":"MEMBER","labels":["Client","Azure.Identity"],"assignees":["m-nash"],"reactions":{"total_count":1,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"hooray":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":1},"created_at":"2026-03-11T21:37:33Z","updated_at":"2026-03-11T21:37:33Z"}

This is by design for the config-driven creation path, but creates a gap for scenarios where developers need to:

1. **Override Transport** — e.g., inject a custom `HttpClientTransport` backed by `IHttpClientFactory`, or use a corporate proxy transport
2. **Add policies** — e.g., logging policies, telemetry policies, or custom retry policies that can't be expressed as config values
3. **Apply to chained credentials** — each source in a `ChainedTokenCredential` has its own options, so customizations need to reach individual credential instances inside the chain

### Current Behavior

- `ClientOptions(IConfigurationSection)` always creates a default transport via `HttpPipelineTransport.Create()` — config has no transport binding
- `HttpPipelineTransport` is abstract and cannot be instantiated from config
- `HttpPipelinePolicy` instances are added via `AddPolicy()` — a programmatic-only API
- `ChainedTokenCredentialFactory` creates credentials directly from `DefaultAzureCredentialOptions` source elements with no parent propagation of Transport or policies

### Desired Behavior

Provide a mechanism for developers to customize Transport and/or add policies to credentials created from configuration. This should work for:

- Single credentials (e.g., `CredentialSource: "AzureCliCredential"`)
- `ChainedTokenCredential` with multiple sources

### Design Considerations

- Should this be a callback/delegate on `DefaultAzureCredentialOptions` that receives each credential's options before construction?
- Should there be a post-config `Action<TokenCredentialOptions>` that applies to all credentials in the chain?
- How does this interact with DI — should `IHttpClientFactory` integration be a first-class pattern?
- `Transport` is not serializable to JSON, so this is inherently a code + config hybrid arguments.body:
## 🎯 Agentic Issue Triage

**Type:** Feature Request
**Component:** `Azure.Identity` — `ConfigurableCredential` / `ChainedTokenCredential`
**Assigned to:** @m-nash

This is a well-scoped feature request to add a programmatic hook for customizing `Transport` and `HttpPipelinePolicy` instances on credentials built from `IConfiguration`. The core gap is that `HttpPipelineTransport` is abstract and cannot be deserialized from config, and `AddPolicy()` is inherently code-only — so a code+config hybrid mechanism is needed.

<details>
<summary>📋 Summary of the Gap</summary>

| Area | Current Limitation |
|---|---|
| `Transport` | `HttpPipelineTransport.Create()` is always called; no config binding |
| `HttpPipelinePolicy` | `AddPolicy()` is programmatic-only, no config path |
| `ChainedTokenCredential` | Each credential in the chain has independent options; no parent propagation |
| DI integration | No first-class `IHttpClientFactory` integration in the credential options path |

</details>

<details>
<summary>💡 Design Ideas & Considerations</summary>

1. **Callback/delegate on options** — A `Action<TokenCredentialOptions>` (or `Action<TokenCredentialOptions, string credentialSourceName>`) callback registered on `DefaultAzureCredentialOptions` / `ChainedTokenCredentialOptions` that fires before each credential is constructed. This is the most flexible and DI-friendly option.

2. **Post-config hook per source** — A dictionary-style API where callers register per-source customizers keyed by `CredentialSource` enum value. More granular but more verbose for "apply to all" scenarios.

3. **`IConfigureOptions<T>` integration (DI)** — Leverage ASP.NET Core's options pattern so that `IConfigureOptions<TokenCredentialOptions>` implementations registered in DI are automatically applied. Natural fit for the `IHttpClientFactory` use case.

4. **Propagation vs. per-credential** — For `ChainedTokenCredential`, decide whether the hook applies to all credentials uniformly (simpler) or per-credential-type (more control, but requires the callback to inspect the options type).

**Recommended starting point:** An `Action<TokenCredentialOptions>` delegate property on `ConfigurableCredentialOptions` / `ChainedTokenCredentialOptions` that receives each credential's options object before the credential is instantiated. This covers Transport injection, policy addition, and is easily testable.

</details>

<details>
<summary>🔗 Relevant Code Locations</summary>

- `sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/src/` — main credential implementations
- `ClientOptions` constructor that accepts `IConfigurationSection` — where transport is hardcoded via `HttpPipelineTransport.Create()`
- `ChainedTokenCredentialFactory` — where credential instances are created from `DefaultAzureCredentialOptions` source elements
- `DefaultAzureCredentialOptions` — options class that would need the new callback property

</details>

<details>
<summary>✅ Suggested Sub-Tasks / Checklist</summary>

- [ ] **API design review** — Agree on the callback/delegate shape (`Action<TokenCredentialOptions>`, typed overloads, or DI-based)
- [ ] **Design for single credential path** — Hook into `ClientOptions(IConfigurationSection)` to call the delegate before transport creation
- [ ] **Design for `ChainedTokenCredential` path** — Propagate the delegate through `ChainedTokenCredentialFactory` to each credential's options
- [ ] **IHttpClientFactory scenario validation** — Write a prototype that injects a transport backed by `IHttpClientFactory` via the new hook to validate the design works end-to-end
- [ ] **Public API listing update** — Run `Export-API.ps1 identity` after implementing
- [ ] **Unit tests** — Cover single credential, chained credential, and DI integration scenarios
- [ ] **README / samples update** — Add a code sample showing the hybrid config+code customization pattern

</details>

<details>
<summary>📚 Useful Resources</summary>

- [Azure.Identity README](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/main/sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/README.md)
- [HttpPipeline and transport customization docs](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/main/sdk/core/Azure.Core/README.md#transport)
- [Azure SDK .NET Design Guidelines — pipeline policies](https://azure.github.io/azure-sdk/dotnet_introduction.html#dotnet-http-pipeline)
- [IHttpClientFactory with Azure SDK blog post](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/azure-sdk/net-framework-compatibility/)

</details>
